Okay, weekly meetings. What are the weekly meetings? Okay, this is a time each week to review the prior week's priorities and hold each other accountable for them. Okay, as well as set the next weeks. So here's an example of how I would structure a weekly meeting. What is the purpose? The purpose is to give clarity to all team members on the company's progress week by week. Okay, so creating weekly commitments that you're working towards. Okay, based on your monthly, quarterly and yearly goals. And the most important part of this meeting is that it serves as almost an accountability session of the week, meaning you can identify who's winning, who's going slow, who needs help, etc. Okay, so the first thing you want to do is make sure that you put this for the same time every week. I suggest doing it on Monday and the same link. It might start at 30 minutes. It might work its way to 60. It might get to 90 at some point. It really depends on the amount of people in there. Okay, one review the agenda. So say we are going to go over these numbers, these numbers, accountability, weekly commitments, etc. Right. Second, you want to share a client and a staff win from the week prior. So you want to start out with like something positive, right, something to like get people engaged to catch their attention to wake them up because it's Monday morning, right. Three is sharing your scoreboard from the week prior. So the same numbers that you share in your daily huddle, you'll share the compilation of them over those seven days prior. And that's what you share on your weekly meeting. Okay, from there, you're going to ask questions about the scoreboard, dig into it. That will often dictate priorities. Okay, so if for some reason sales were down, then you're going to say, okay, well then it means a priority to get those up. What can we do? Let's look through the pipeline. Do we have enough leads? Do we need to make new creative? Do we need to do this? What is that? And then assigning priorities to different people based on their positions. After that, you want to review last week's commitments versus the results. So what did you commit to last week? Did it actually get done, right, and then identifying any critical issues? Something I want to point out is that if somebody doesn't complete something, it doesn't mean that you are mean to them, but you do hold them accountable. And so you would say in front of the group because you want the respect of the rest of the group. Hey, so that was supposed to be completed on Friday. Everyone else on the team did get it done by Friday. Can you share with us why you didn't? And so if you listen, I don't sound mean when I'm saying that because my tone is just neutral. And so if you are asking people why they didn't get something done, ensure that it's neutral and that you're not emotional about it. Okay, because it is the worst thing you can do is make assumptions about why they didn't get it done. Instead, you need to investigate. So investigate, do not assume. Then go through your calendar for the week. What do we have coming up? Do we have an event in two weeks? We have an event this coming weekend. Do we have a nutrition orientation on Saturday that we need to make sure we're trained up for? What is it coming up? And how's that going to dictate our priorities of the week? Lastly, you want to establish deliverables for each team member for the week. So each person leaves knowing exactly what's on their list for the week and then what their deadlines are. And lastly, you want to recap and close, meaning review everything, just kind of summarize what you said during the meeting. Like, hey, guys, so we realized last week we kind of slacked here. So this week, here's what we're going to do differently. And Sarah, you're going to do this. Sally's doing this. Josh is doing this. Boom, let's go. Crush it, guys. Okay, that's all. That's what you need for your weekly meeting. As you continue to have these, you can delegate pieces of them to your team. So maybe your sales manager is responsible for slide number four, filling out the numbers ahead of time. Maybe your operations manager runs the entire meeting. This will change over time. Just go with your gut. So make sure that you keep it refreshing. Make sure that you keep it focused and make sure that you keep it on point.
